clinical_information.diagnosis: Infantile spasms (West syndrome), with new atonic seizure activity and concern for evolution to Lennox-Gastaut syndrome
clinical_information.prior_treatments_and_results: Patient is adherent to vigabatrin and a ketogenic diet. Despite these therapies, the patient has had increased spasm frequency overnight and development of new atonic seizure semiology (sudden limp episodes with head drop); two spasms were witnessed on exam. Current therapies are inadequate to control seizures.
clinical_information.lab_summary: Overall CBC and CMP are within reference ranges. The only consistent abnormality is a slightly low MCH (23 pg; ref 24–30) with a borderline low MCV (72 fL; ref 70–86) while hemoglobin (11.5 g/dL) and hematocrit are in the reported normal ranges — a pattern suggesting mild microcytosis/hypochromia. This can be seen with early iron deficiency, thalassemia trait, or other microcytic processes. All electrolytes, renal and liver function tests, albumin, and proteins are normal. Consider targeted follow-up such as iron studies (ferritin, serum iron, TIBC), peripheral smear, reticulocyte count, and hemoglobin electrophoresis if clinically indicated.
clinical_information.imaging_results: EEG (30-minute recording, awake): Background rhythm shows mixed theta and delta activity with predominant generalized slowing; sleep cycles not achieved. Interictal: slow spike-and-wave discharges at 1.5–2.5 Hz, predominantly frontal and central; widespread background slowing with occasional bursts of rhythmic delta. Ictal: several brief generalized seizure episodes characterized by multifocal spikes followed by slow waves, correlating with reported infantile spasms. Occasional generalized slow wave discharges suggestive of atonic seizures. Photostimulation produced a mild photoparoxysmal response with increased slow wave activity but no definite photically induced seizures. Some muscle artifact during movement. Impression: EEG features consistent with Lennox-Gastaut syndrome with evidence of infantile spasms and possible atonic seizures.
clinical_information.symptom_severity_and_impact: Increased seizure frequency and new, more varied seizure types (atonic episodes with head drops and continued infantile spasms). Patient appears fatigued; two spasms were witnessed during exam. Presentation required hospital admission and pediatric neurology consultation.
clinical_information.prognosis_and_risk_if_not_approved: Clinical course is concerning for progression to Lennox-Gastaut syndrome if seizures remain uncontrolled. Continued uncontrolled and more varied seizures pose risk of further neurologic compromise and developmental impact.
clinical_information.clinical_rationale_for_urgency: Seizures have worsened in frequency and semiology despite current therapy, with new atonic events and persistent infantile spasms; patient is admitted and pediatric neurology recommends initiating Epidiolex urgently while further diagnostic evaluation (24-hour video EEG, possible MRI) is performed.

treatment_request.dosage: 100 mg/mL; Take 2.5mg/kg/day divided in two doses for one week, then increase to 5mg/kg/day divided in two doses.
treatment_request.rationale: Diagnosis: Lennox-Gastaut Syndrome. Current therapy with Vigabatrin and ketogenic diet is insufficient.

several different types of seizures, most commonly atonic seizures (sudden loss of 
muscle tone and limpn ess) and  tonic seizures .6,9  The three main forms of treatment 
of Lennox -Gastaut syndrome  are ASMs, dietary therapy (typically the ketogenic diet), 
and device/surgery (e .g., vagus n erve stimulation, corpus callos tomy) .9  None of the 
therapies are effective in all cases of Lennox -Gastaut syndrome  and the disorder has 
proven pa rticularly resistan t to most therapeutic options.  
 
Tuberous sclerosis complex is a rare, genetic disease that causes non -cancerous

heart, eyes, lungs, and skin.10  It c

considers v alproic acid and clobazam to be the first -line treatment for Dravet 
syndrome.4  If seizure control is suboptimal, Diacomit and topiramate are second -
line treatment . Ketogenic diet is moderately effective and can also be considered 
second -line. The Dravet Foundation states that Diacomit , Epidiolex , and Fintepla  are 
considered first -line agents for the treatment of Dravet syndrome.2  If control is still 
inadequate, other therapies to consider are clonazepam, levetiracetam, and 
zonisamide.2,4  Sodium channel blockers (e.g., carbamazepine, oxcarbazepine, 
lamotrigine, and phenytoin ) can worsen seizures in Dravet syndrome . Additionally, 
vigabatrin and tiagabine may increase the frequency of myoclonic seizures and should 
be avoided.  
 
Lennox -Gastaut Syndrome  
Currently, the FDA -approved drugs for this condition  are Epidiolex, Fintepla, 
felbamate , Banzel® (rufinamide tablet, oral suspension), lamotrigine , topiramate, and 
clobazam .12  Despite the lack of level I or level II evidence, valproic acid remains a 
mainstay in treatment.8,9,13  If valproic acid doe

epilepsy and were on stable doses of ASMs for 4 weeks before starting Epidiolex as 
add-on therapy . The initial dose of Epidiolex was 2 to 10 mg/kg/day (taken as two 
divided doses) and gradually titrated until intolerance or to a maximum dose of 25 
mg/kg/day or 50 mg/kg/day, depending upon treatment site . Of those enrolled in 
the expanded access program, 892 patients were evalu ated and included in the safety 
analysis set and 840 patients were included in the efficacy analysis set . Through 192 
weeks, the m edian percentage reduction in seizure frequency across visit windows 
ranged from 50% to 67% for convulsive seizures and 46% to 66% for total seizures . 
In a cohort of 132 patients (72 children, 60 adults) with treatment -resistant epilepsy, 
bi-weekly seizure frequency decreased from a mean of 144.4 at entry to 52.2 at 12 
weeks (P = 0.01) and remained stable thereafter.15  Of note, patients with a diagnosis 
of Lennox -Gastaut syndrome  or Dravet syndrome  were initially excluded because of 
preferential enrollment into the randomized clinical trials; once these trials were 
closed for enrollment, patients with these syndromes were also enrolled.  In a

heart, eyes, lungs, and skin.10  It can result in a combination of symptoms including 
seizures, impaired intellectual development, autism, behavioral problems, skin 
abnormalities, and kidney disease . Seizures affect most individuals with tuberous 
sclerosis complex  at some point during their life  and can be difficult to control.  
 
Clinical Efficacy  in Other Refractory Seizures  
In 2014, an expanded access program was initiated to provide Epidiolex to patients 
with treatment -resistant epilepsy.14  Of the 840 patients included in a published 
review, 1 92 patients were diagnosed with D ravet syndrome  or Lennox -Gastaut 
syndrome , and 648 patients were diagnosed with other conditions, including CDKL5 
deficiency disorder, Dup15q, Aicardi, and Doose syndromes; febrile infection ‐related 
epilepsy syndromes; tuberous sclerosis complex; Sturge ‐Weber syndrome; 
lissencephaly; cortical malformatio n/dysplasia; and myoclonic absence.  The patients 
enrolled in the study had severe, intractable, childhood -onset treatment -resistant 
epilepsy and were on stable doses of ASMs for 4 weeks before starting Epidiolex as

decision.reason: The submitted request is for Epidiolex to treat Lennox-Gastaut syndrome (LGS). The provided policy text identifies Epidiolex as an FDA-approved drug for Lennox-Gastaut syndrome and describes use of Epidiolex as add-on therapy in patients with treatment-resistant epilepsy who remained on stable antiseizure medications. The patient has a documented diagnosis of LGS with worsening seizures despite current therapy (vigabatrin and ketogenic diet), and the requested dosing (start 2.5 mg/kg/day then increase to 5 mg/kg/day) falls within the typical initial dosing range described in the policy. Therefore, the request is consistent with the policy-authorized indication and dosing for add-on therapy in LGS.

Lennox -Gastaut syndrome, a severe epileptic and developmental encephalopathy, is 
associated with a high rate of morbidity and mortality .6,7  Lennox -Gastaut syndrome
